text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS AND ORAL CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 58-year-old male with stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma, KRAS G12D-mutant, pMMR/MSS. Synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases. Currently on modified FOLFOX-6 plus bevacizumab, now completing Cycle 8. Oxaliplatin dose reduced 25% since Cycle 7 for cumulative Grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy. Prior CT abdomen/pelvis after Cycle 4 demonstrated partial hepatic and primary tumor response. Repeat imaging for interval response assessment and surgical resectability planning.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed following administration of oral contrast and 120 mL IV iohexol. Arterial, portal venous, and delayed phase acquisitions of the abdomen obtained. Axial, coronal, and sagittal reformats reviewed. Images compared to prior CT abdomen/pelvis (post-Cycle 4 study) and original staging CT.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs: The lung parenchyma is clear. No focal consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax. No suspicious pulmonary nodules identified. Airways are patent to segmental level bilaterally. No endobronchial lesions.

Mediastinum/Hila: Mediastinal contours are unremarkable. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. No pericardial effusion. Heart size normal. Aorta unremarkable in caliber. Mild atherosclerotic calcification of the thoracic aorta.

Pleura: No pleural thickening or effusion.

Chest Wall/Bones: Port-a-cath catheter tip identified in the right chest wall with tip projecting over the cavoatrial junction -- appropriate positioning. No osseous destructive lesions in the visualized thorax.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: There has been further interval decrease in the size of the previously characterized hepatic metastases. All four lesions remain identifiable:

  (1) Segment VI (dominant right lobe lesion): Now measures approximately 2.0 x 1.7 cm in axial dimension (previously 2.6 x 2.2 cm after Cycle 4, and 3.2 x 2.8 cm at baseline). The lesion continues to demonstrate hypoenhancement relative to the surrounding hepatic parenchyma on portal venous phase, with a rim of faint peripheral enhancement. Appearance is consistent with ongoing treatment response; no central liquefaction or new arterial hyperenhancement to suggest dedifferentiation.

  (2) Segment IVb lesion: Now measures approximately 0.9 x 0.8 cm (previously 1.3 x 1.2 cm after Cycle 4). Continued reduction in size. Lesion is barely perceptible on arterial phase and demonstrates mild hypoenhancement on portal venous imaging.

  (3) Segment II (left lobe): Now measures approximately 0.7 x 0.6 cm (previously 1.0 x 0.9 cm after Cycle 4). Near the lower limit of reliable characterization on CT. Subtle hypoenhancing focus; continued diminution consistent with favorable treatment response.

  (4) Segment VII lesion: Measures approximately 0.5 x 0.4 cm. Near the limits of CT resolution. Previously 0.7 x 0.6 cm. Tiny hypoattenuating focus -- difficult to confidently characterize at this size; cannot exclude near-complete radiographic response.

Overall hepatic parenchymal background: The remaining liver parenchyma demonstrates heterogeneous enhancement with mild periportal edema and subtle perisinusoidal haziness, which may reflect chemotherapy-associated hepatic parenchymal injury (sinusoidal obstruction syndrome/hepatic sinusoidal toxicity) in the setting of prolonged oxaliplatin-containing regimen. No biliary ductal dilatation. Portal vein patent with normal hepatopetal flow suggested on portal venous phase. Hepatic veins patent.

Gallbladder/Biliary System: Gallbladder is unremarkable without cholelithiasis, wall thickening, or pericholecystic fluid. Common bile duct not dilated (measured 4 mm).

Pancreas: Normal attenuation and morphology. No pancreatic ductal dilatation, no peripancreatic stranding, no focal masses.

Spleen: Mildly enlarged at 13.4 cm in craniocaudal dimension (previously 12.8 cm), which may be related to portal hypertension from bevacizumab-related vascular effects or chemotherapy-associated hepatic congestion. No splenic focal lesions.

Adrenal Glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are symmetric and unremarkable. No nodules or masses.

Kidneys: Both kidneys enhance symmetrically with normal corticomedullary differentiation. No hydronephrosis. No renal masses. Mild bilateral renal cortical thinning compatible with age-related change.

Retroperitoneum/Aorta: No retroperitoneal adenopathy. Aorta normal caliber with mild atherosclerotic changes. No aneurysmal dilatation.

PELVIS:

Sigmoid Colon/Primary Tumor: There is persistent circumferential mural thickening of the sigmoid colon, now measuring approximately 3.1 x 2.7 cm in maximal cross-sectional dimension (previously 3.6 x 3.2 cm after Cycle 4). Mural enhancement is somewhat heterogeneous. The degree of pericolonic fat stranding appears mildly reduced compared to the staging CT, though some residual haziness persists. The lesion does not appear to involve adjacent structures; fat planes with the anterior sacral fascia and posterior bladder wall appear preserved.

Lymph Nodes: The pericolonic nodal burden is markedly reduced. No pericolonic lymph nodes are now convincingly enlarged by size criteria. Previously noted 0.9 cm perimesenteric node is no longer distinctly visualized. Pelvic sidewall nodes are not enlarged.

Bladder/Rectum: Urinary bladder is decompressed but without wall thickening or mass. Rectum is unremarkable.

Bony Pelvis: No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions to suggest skeletal metastatic involvement.

Peritoneum: No ascites. No nodular peritoneal thickening or soft tissue implants along the peritoneal surfaces. No omental caking identified.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Continued favorable treatment response in all hepatic metastatic deposits compared to post-Cycle 4 imaging and original staging CT. The dominant Segment VI right lobe lesion has approximately halved in size from baseline (now 2.0 x 1.7 cm from 3.2 x 2.8 cm). The Segment IVb and Segment II lesions are substantially reduced. The Segment VII lesion has diminished to subcentimeter size and approaches radiographic complete response.

2. Mild further reduction in the sigmoid primary tumor (now 3.1 x 2.7 cm from 3.6 x 3.2 cm at Cycle 4 imaging), with decreased pericolonic nodal burden; pericolic lymph nodes no longer identified.

3. No new pulmonary nodules, no new hepatic lesions, no peritoneal carcinomatosis, and no osseous metastatic disease -- no evidence of new sites of spread.

4. Mild hepatic parenchymal changes (perisinusoidal haziness, borderline splenomegaly) warrant clinical correlation with liver function tests; findings potentially consistent with chemotherapy-associated hepatic parenchymal injury following eight cycles of oxaliplatin-containing regimen.

5. Port-a-cath tip in satisfactory position.

CEA: 38.1 ng/mL (per clinical data provided; decreased from 67.4 ng/mL at Cycle 4 assessment and 148.2 ng/mL at baseline).

Multidisciplinary hepatobiliary surgical evaluation is suggested given the distribution of residual hepatic disease and degree of response achieved.
